description: Multi-topic pivot: starts with logging, discovers performance issue, pivots to optimization

content.text: Add debug logging to the API request handler so we can see what data is coming in.
I'll add debug logging to the request handler. Let me find the handler module first.

defmodule Myapp.APIHandler do
  require Logger
  
  def handle_request(conn) do
    data = parse_body(conn)
    result = process_data(data)
    send_response(conn, result)
  end
  
  defp process_data(data) do
    data
    |> Enum.map(&transform/1)
    |> Enum.filter(&valid?/1)
    |> calculate_result()
  end
end
I'll add logging at the start of handle_request to log incoming data.

args.new_content:
def handle_request(conn) do
    data = parse_body(conn)
    Logger.debug("Received API request with data: #{inspect(data)}")

content.text: Good. But wait, I'm seeing the logs now and we're processing HUGE arrays - like 10,000 items per request. That seems like a problem.

That does sound concerning! Processing 10,000 items synchronously in a web request could cause timeouts. Let me look at the process_data function more closely to understand what operations we're doing.
The transform function does some JSON parsing and the valid? function makes a database query to check if the item exists. So yeah, that's 10k database queries per request!
That's definitely the issue! We're doing N+1 queries. Let me refactor this to batch the validation queries. Instead of checking items one by one, we should fetch all the IDs in a single query.

Full file:
defmodule Myapp.APIHandler do
  require Logger
  alias Myapp.Repo
  alias Myapp.Item
  
  def handle_request(conn) do
    data = parse_body(conn)
    Logger.debug("Received API request with data: #{inspect(data)}")
    result = process_data(data)
    send_response(conn, result)
  end
  
  defp process_data(data) do
    data
    |> Enum.map(&transform/1)
    |> Enum.filter(&valid?/1)
    |> calculate_result()
  end
  
  defp transform(item), do: Jason.decode!(item)
  
  defp valid?(item) do
    Repo.exists?(from i in Item, where: i.id == ^item["id"])
  end
end

I see the problem clearly now. Instead of checking each item individually with valid?, we should transform all items first, extract all the IDs, query for them in one batch, and then filter. Let me refactor process_data.

args.old_content:
defp process_data(data) do
    data
    |> Enum.map(&transform/1)
    |> Enum.filter(&valid?/1)
    |> calculate_result()
  end
  
  defp transform(item), do: Jason.decode!(item)
  
  defp valid?(item) do
    Repo.exists?(from i in Item, where: i.id == ^item["id"])
  end
args.new_content:
defp process_data(data) do
    transformed = Enum.map(data, &transform/1)
    valid_ids = get_valid_ids(transformed)
    
    transformed
    |> Enum.filter(fn item -> MapSet.member?(valid_ids, item["id"]) end)
    |> calculate_result()
  end
  
  defp transform(item), do: Jason.decode!(item)
  
  defp get_valid_ids(items) do
    ids = Enum.map(items, & &1["id"])
    
    from(i in Item, where: i.id in ^ids, select: i.id)
    |> Repo.all()
    |> MapSet.new()
  end

content.text: Much better! Now it's a single query. But should we also paginate or set a limit on the incoming request size?
Yes, that's a good defensive practice. Let me add a check at the start of handle_request to reject requests with too many items.

args.old_content:
def handle_request(conn) do
    data = parse_body(conn)
    Logger.debug("Received API request with data: #{inspect(data)}")
    result = process_data(data)
    send_response(conn, result)
  end
args.new_content:
@max_items 1000
  
  def handle_request(conn) do
    data = parse_body(conn)
    Logger.debug("Received API request with #{length(data)} items")
    
    if length(data) > @max_items do
      send_error(conn, "Request exceeds maximum of #{@max_items} items")
    else
      result = process_data(data)
      send_response(conn, result)
    end
  end

content.text: Perfect! This should handle both the performance issue and prevent abuse. Good catch on the N+1 problem.
You're welcome! The debug logging helped us discover a serious performance issue. The refactoring reduces 10,000 queries to just 1, and the size limit prevents future abuse.
